g CURRENT AFFAIRS.

““On Fontenoy, on Fontenoy!'” There is a branch
of the ““Irish National Foresters’’ in Dublin, and it
is called the ‘‘ Fontenoy’' Branch. It held its first
annual battle to the tune of ‘“ Revenge, Remember
Limerick, dash down the Sassanagh,”” at Wynn’s Hotel

recently.

‘“Lord Bung, he says, you have your wish, there are
your Saxon foes,
The marshal almost smiles to see so furiously he goes.””

That ot not a correct: quotution, but somehow with
the programme of the ‘ Fontenoy'’ smoking concert
before us. we could not keep Bung out of it. We are

told by the ‘‘Fontenoys’ on page 1 of their pro-
gramme—‘ You will be interested to know that the
best drinks obtainable may be had at P’ ““Hark
to that fierce huzza!”  Another bungery owner
aunounces ‘“ Whiskey a speciality.””  On another of
tho ‘‘heights’’ of the Forester ¢ Fontenoy’’ Pro-
gramme the thirsty green ones are reminded of a certain
whiskey that is sold in Great Britain Street, of another
Bung in East Arran Street, of a Bung on Usher’s
Quay, ‘‘ The noted house for whiskey,”” of a Bung on
Summer Hill. Turning over the page we find Alder-
man Davin announcing himself, a bungery in Suffolk
Street, another in Talbot Street, yet another in Great
Britain Street, another announces ‘¢ first-class drinks
only ”’ in Marlboro’ Street, yet one other in Britain
Street, another bungery in Mary Street, ‘‘all drinks
guaranteed the best.””  There are two pages more of
the green printed clip of ‘ Fontenoy.”” We find in
them a Bung from the North Strand, another in South
King Street, another in Summer Hill and Gardiner
Street, another in Capel Street for ‘‘ all-round, reliable
drinks.”’  As a diversion we may remark that the green
Foresters of ‘‘ Fontenoy ’’ are requested by a firm in
Little Mary Street to call there for ‘evening dress
suits for sale or hire.”” Well, if any of them called
we hope they did so in the first instance, and did not
wait until they paid their respects and their coppers to
all the Bungs who invited them to sample their special
ammunition for the ‘ Fontenoy ’ heroes. =~ We come
back to Bung—‘‘ Come with the crowd to where the
creamy pint is to be had, , Great Britain Street,
second to none in Dublin.”” Yet another bungery from
Summer Hill, another in Marlboro’ Street, and the last
we-counted in Amiens Street. As the smoking concert:
was held on licensed premises, we suppose the ‘‘ Fon-
tenoy ”’ Foresters were not confined to Irish plug and
minerals during the entertainment, so that if they called
to all these places afterwards it lends a new significance
to the announcement—‘‘To provide against Sickness,
Distress and Death join the Irish National Foresters”
Benefit Society. Branch ‘ Fontenoy’ (No. 590) meets
at 67 Summer Hill, ete.”” Certainly after such a pro-
gramme one would do well to provide against ‘‘sick-
ness, distress and death.”” There is no mention of an
Irish song on the programme—just as well, perhaps,
not to have Irish mixed up with all these Bungs. One
performer was down to sing ‘‘ Pearl of Ceylon,”
another for ‘‘ Comic song and dance,”’ called ‘‘ Ma
Josie,”” but a Mr. R. J. Kelly is on the programme for
a recitation, ‘‘ Fontenoy.”” We do not see that anyone
sang ‘“ The Boys of Wexford.”” Perhaps the ‘‘ Fon-
tenoy ”’ Foresters might have their conscience troubled
by the lines:—

‘“ We bravely fought and conquered at Ross and Wex-
ford town,

And if we failed to keep them ’twas drink that brought
us down ;

We had no drink beside us on Tubberneering’s day,

Depending on the long, bright pike, and well it worked
its way.”

And some of the samplers of the Bungs on the pro-
gramme might prefer not to hear:—

“’Twas drink that still betrayed us, of them we had
no fear,

For every man could do his part like Forth and Shelma-
lier;

My curs)e upon all drinking, it made our hearts full
sore

For bra;w;ery won each battle, but drink lost evermore.”

‘ 178

This programme of the ‘‘Fontenoy '’ Foresters is a
disgrace. Recently the Foresters of Newry presented
a political address in Irish; what a difference between
the Newry branch and the ‘‘ Fontenoy’’ of Summer
Hill. Humbugs and- ranters who prate about ‘‘ Fon-
tenoy > and things of that sort are fairly familiar to
us all. We are not familiar with the °‘TForesters’’’
constitution, but surely there ought to be some central
committee or authority to deal with such blots on it as
the “ Fontenoys’’’ programme.

The following complaint from Mr. P. J. O'Driscoll
has been sent to the L.G.B. Auditor at present engaged
on the accounts of the Rathmines Urban District:—
“ Dear Sir—I hereby object to your completing the pre-
sent audit of the accounts of the Rathmines Urban Dis-
trict Council—.e., signing the auditor’s certificate—till
all books, vouchers, and other documents relating to the
accounts shall have been placed for seven days in the
Town MHall for the ratepayers’ inspection, also till the
fact that they are thus open for inspection is duly
advertised, and, further, till you (after the period of
inspection) again sit at the Town Hall to hear any
objection the ratepayers may feel called upon to lay
before you as per the regulations of the Local Govern-
ment Board. You are aware that the vouchers have
not yet been produced to the ratepayers, and that the
regulation of your Board regarding this most important
matter in connection with the audit, and also the
Board’s letter of December 15th, 1906, have been
flagrantly ignored by the Urban District Council. The
value of a T..G.B. audit of this exclusive Council’s
ac]c&ou’n,ts will be apparent from the action you will
take.

In another column will be found an article of great
practical importance to all ratepayers who suffer under
Boards that squander their money. If an article such
as that which® Mr. Sullivan writes on the political and
sectarian Urban District Council of Rathmines could
be written of some Nationalist Board, what a flare of
bugles there would be in the English press, and how the
‘“Irish Times’’ would thump the big drum, and how
Lord Ardilaun’s ‘“ Double X-Press’’ would squeal on
the tin whistle; and no doubt Lord Ashpit’s ‘ griev-
ances ’’ would contain some shovelfuls of it. We await
with interest Mr. King’s ruling on Mr. O’Driscoll’s
objection.

There was a concert on the Feast of All Saints at
Castleknock College. We have a programme of it
before us. The gentle reader thinks already that the
College is about to get what we all often got when we
were young, and when, no doubt, we deserved it—our
““one, two, three.”” The gentle reader is mistaken.
‘Amongst the items on the programme of this recent
concert at Castleknock are two songs in Irish—*‘ Peapla

- an Dpoltarg Bain’ sung by Seamup O Nualldin,
and “4n Cpuig,” sung by Tomdp Mac didpein.
Other items are a four-hand reel, an eight-hand reel, a
double jig.

Last year Miceal O Fostuoa presented a Postal
Order for payment at Phibsborough Branch P.O.
(Dublin), but he was refused payment unless he signed
the order according to the English form of his name.
He was also refused payment at the G.P.O., Dublin,
under like circumstances. Mr. O Fogluva wrote to
the G.P.O., London, and to the G.P.0O., Dublin. In
November of last year a letter was received from the
Money Order Department, G.P.O., London, in which

THE LEADER.

NoveMBER 9, 1907.

the Controller stated:— I am directed by the Post-
master-General to inform you that from inquiries that
have been made in the matter, it has been ascertained
that payment of the Order in question was refused at
the Phibsborough and Dublin Post Offices under a mis-
apprehension. Suitable notice has been ba}{eu of 'the
matter with the officers concerned, and instructions
issued as to the proper method of dealing with similar
cases in fubure.””

That was in last November. A month or so ago
Micesl O Poxluoa was again refused cash for Postal
Order signed in Irish at the Phibsborough Post Office.
Necdless to say, our sturdy Irish-Ireland friend did not
let it go with the Post Office this time either. On the
6th September he received from London the usual red
tape acknowledgment of his first letter, and on the 4th
September from Dublin, But London addressed him
correctly, whilst Mr. R. A. Egerton, the imported Sec.
at Dublin, turned his name into English. On th> 17th
September the following letter came from the Dublin
Office :—*‘ In reply to your letter of the 3rd inst., I am
divected by the Postmaster-General to express.regret
that payment of the Postal Orders to which you refer
was refused at Phibsborough Post Office under a mis-
apprehension.  Suitable notice has becen taken of the
irregularity.’”

But almost a year previously the Post Office also
stated that ‘“ Suitable notice has heen taken of the
matter with the officers concerned, etc.”” Yet the
trouble occurred again quite Tecently! Amnother point
arises. If Phibsborough Post Office must cash P.O.’s
signed in Irish, on what principle does the Secretary of
the Post Office in Dublin take it on himself to English
the name of the gentleman to whom he had to apologise
for the bungling of his officials in Phibsborough? Tt is
admitted that they were wrong in not recognising the
Irish signature; how is it that Mr. Egerton changes
that Trish name into its English form when apologising
for the mistake of his officials? Our vigorous Irish-
Irelander wanted to know the why and wherefore of
this changing of his name by the Post Office, and wrote
on the 4th of Octcber. On the 7th October Mr. R. A.
Egerton, Secretary, formally acknowledged receipt of
the letter; but, so far as we know, he has not up to
this offered any explanation, or attempted any justifi-
cation, for tampering with an Irish name. Tt is signi-
ficant and satisfactory that the acknowledgment of the
letter was addressed to Miceal O Fogluda.

We are informed that at the meeting of the New-
castle West District Council, at which the advertisement
stipulating for British matiresses was passed, the Clerk
was directed to advertise for ‘“ American Bacon,’’ it
being alleced . that someone stated that he could not
cub up Irish bacon! The procedure adopted at these
mectings of the District Council at Newcastle West is—
the master reports that he wants a certain article or
articles, and an order is given to advertise; the master
writes the advertisement and passes it on to the Clerk
for publication. The District Councillors are respon-
sible, but apparently their responsibility lies easily on
them, for we understand that they usually leave things
of this kind to the master’s discretion. In the case of
the now notorious mattresses advertisement that has
given Newcastle West District Council an unpleasant
natoriety all over the country, and outside the country,

we believe that the master applied for the particular

kind of mattresses, so that the D.C.’s present knew all
about the matter. We hear that fresh advertisements

“Palmam qgui meruit ferat.”

At the matinee at the Abbey Theatre on Saturday 
the curtain was raised to the accompaniment of slow 
music. 
They are getting on-or should we say, going 
back! 
" DervorgiHa/' a tragedy in one act, by Lady 
Gregory, is the best play that we have yet seen at the 
Abbey. Indeed, could we have conveniently clone so 
we would have gone to the night performance on Satur-
day also. It is pleasant, if only for a change, to be 
a?le. to s1:1-y some~hing sweeping and without qualifica~ 
tion of a play at the Abbey, or at any other place. We 
won't dilate on the history in the play ; there are some 
who blame Dervorgilla, once queen of Breffui, for 
having brought " the Non:nau o'er," and there are some 
who dispute that view; they can all take their choice 
so far as we are concern(ld, as we are not historians h1 
the ~euse of getting excited over such remote questions 
of fact . The play for one reason or another recalled 
" Everyman " to our minds. 
_ The acting all round 
strnck us as superb . We will not say again that Vv. G. 
Fay is always W . G. Fay, and that he can only act 
broad comedy. 
He showed himself as an actor, in q1-1ite 
a new light as a wandering songmaker of the twelftp. 
century. l\tiiss Allgood as '' Dervorgilla ' ' added to her 
already 
establish.eel 
reputation. 
The whole 
play

a study in remorse, but a beautiful study withal, and it 
is the finest thing we have seen for a long time.

The Parish Priest at l\tienlough is Father Colgan,

Miss Barry, now instructing in

For over a year a Home Brightening Scheme was in 
operation in lVIenlough, and domestic science, suited -to 
the circumstances of the district, was taught both in 
the schools and in the homes of the people. 
Classes were 
~ ·also held' in a model cottage occupied by the iustruc-
tresses, and the model cottage was open, to all comers. 
There were some school gardens laid out in Menlough 
also, carpentry classes were established for young men 
and boys, and a lace class. for the girls. 
The Irish 
language 
was, and, we presume, still is, taught

. There is, perhaps, nothing breeds apathy more deadl
than the fact of having it drummedpintoyour ears da;
after day that we are apathetic. Like the Irishman
abroad who read one of Lever's novels and went out
and got drunk and kicked up a row so as to live up to
thg part of Irishman as set forth in the book, so all
this . talk of being apathetic and being a * distressful
country *’ inevitably creates a sort of humour to play
the part.  We are pretty well sick of all classes of
- specialised politicians—with all their noise the chief tan-
+  gible results appear to be mere talk and bungle and an
odd scuffle at a meeting—and one side is about as un-
satisfactory as the others, and that is saying a great
deal. William the screecher plays the ‘“ apathy >’ card
for a great deal more than it is worth. William is
naturally annoyed that the U.I.L. didn’t die within a
month after his ‘‘ resignation.”” The U.L.L. got on so
well without him that he ceased to be resigned to his
resignation, so he started again! Now, as he is not on
top, he paints the state of the country in all the darkest
colours. We are all ‘“apathetic’’ and the rest of it,
an’ it please you, because we have too much sense to
become excited at William’s suppressed screeches.
Edward VIII., of the ‘‘ National Council,” keeps say-
ing nothing, which, no doubt, is about as wise a
thing as he could do; Mr. Redmond is having various
demonstrations which, someway or other, do not appear
to be significant of anything positive; their negative
significance is that the country would rather put up
with Mr. John Redmond than suffer Screeching Wil-
liam at the helm; and Edward VIII. is now head of
a sort of Irish Thames Valley Legitimatist Club, or
- whatever they call it,

All the time whilst screeching William wants to make
us believe that we are gone to the dogs, and whilst
others wish to include us, and other sensible people,
within the circle of their oratorical ‘‘overwhelming
majorities,”” we, and other sensible people, are minding
our business and are disgusted with the wranglers in the
specialised political field. A plague on all your houses.

Now we are convinced that the country, in spite of
the wrangling politicians, is on the mending hand. Our
own pages for many months back have given various
glimpses into constructive activities of various kinds
that are going ahead in many parts of the country.
There is a great deal of the noise about ““ unity ”’ that
reminds us of the man that kicks up most disturbance
at a meeting by shouting out ‘‘order, order.” Let
them split up to their heart’s content if they like ; if the
country gave our various political fishwives the cold
£ shoulder they would soon get tired barging at one

another.  These politicians are spoiled children; as
4 soon as they commence to cry and bite one another all
of us are supposed to upset ourselves on their account
and disorganise the country in the interests of ‘‘unity’” !
We are all to get excited, even though in one section
we find some of our public men so large-minded that
Mr. Thomas Sexton is not on speaking terms with Mr.
John Redmond. Spoiled children is the name for a
lot of them, and what they want is a good spanking and
to be placed in punishment by the nurse—the public.
In our view Mr. John Redmond is about the only man
who shows capacity for the post of chairman of the
grown-up children like William screeching and Tim
grinning and making faces in his tent. No doubt

NoveMBER 9, 1907

Tim likes ‘“ the other bold boy ** to be getting spanked
now ; he has already ‘‘ had some,’’ as the Cockneys say !
Mr. Dolan, the unresigned, who is an M.P. and yet is
against going to Westminster, wants to spend his Elec-
tion Fund on an industry now we see; that might be a
convenient way of retreating from his impending defeat,
but do not the subscribers expect to see some feathers
flying for their money. Had Mr. Dolan made a collec-
tion for an industry, we are sorry to have to say he
would not, in our opinion, have got such a generous
response. Such is the perversity of our childlike public.
People expect to see an election ‘scrap’’ in North
Leitrim, and Mr. Dolan’s importance will dwindle into
his boots, we expect, if he puts the election fund into a
laundry works or a toy factory in Manorhamilton.

We received quite a large number of articles
concerning various towns in response to our note last
week. Indeed, so many did we receive that we think
it would be better for us to name a town to be written
about. However, we will leave well enough alone for
the present. =~ We publish this week an article on
Adare, in the County of Limerick, and next week we
shall publish one on Drogheda. A reader sent us up
a brief article on Mullingar which we also publish this
week, but he does not wish it to come within our
scheme, so Mullingar may still be written of. Another
gentleman sent us up some interesting notes on Tulla-
more in like manner, and we hope to publish them
next week.

In another column Father P. Murphy, M.S.S., of
Enniscorthy, contributes an interesting article on the
Great Temperance Crusade just concluded in Wexford
County.  Father Murphy, who is, and has been for
years, a prominent figure in the Gaelic League, heads
his article: ‘“ Wexford Again Leads.”” This heading
should act as a friendly challenge to other counties, and
we hope that the perusal of what has recently been
done in Wexford will stimulate other counties to similar
endeavours.

Our readers will read with deep regret of the death
of Mr. James Hallissey, one of the best of our Irish
traditional singers. He was always a welcome figure
at Irish-Ireland concerts, and he took part in various
Irish plays. A few years ago he won the first prize
for singing in Irish at the Feis Ceoil. Mr. Hallissey
was only 27 years of age, and his untimely death cuts
short a career of great promise, and is a heavy loss to
the Irish-Treland Movement.

IRISHISATION, AND HOW '1I8 DONE.
ATHER MATHEW MAGUIRE is a convinced
Trish-Trelander, and one could not live several
days in his house without finding that he had pondered
deeply and long over the problem of Irish Revival.
The language is to him, as it is to us all, chiefly a means
to many ends. If he hadn’t the Irish language as a
help he would find his other work impossible.  He
might get up cookery classes, and evening schools and
home brightening schemes, but they wouldn’t march;
so he is convinced, and I thoroughly agree with him,
the language is the inspiration of, and also the factor
that gives staying power to, his schemes.

There is in existence a committee of ladies and gentle-
men which includes on its roll Sir Horace Plunkett,
Lady Arnott, Father T. A. Finlay, Lady Fingal,
and Lady Mayo, and others. This committee
helped Father Maguire when he was a curate
working in  ““Irish Dromore.” He also re-
ceived the committee’s help when he took charge of
the parish of Kilskeery. This committee has funds; let
me hasten to explain—for every Irish ear cocks up at
the mention of the magic word ‘ funds”’—that I under-
stand the funds are subscribed by themselves. There is
nothing whatever to prevent other people forming such
committees, and having funds of their own! Well,
this committee lent a hand to Father Maguire. In
February they sent to his parish Miss K. M. Adrien,
a distinguished pupil of the College of Domestic
Economy in Kildare St., Dublin— she is now by the way
an_enthusiastic studen of Irish—and she remained in
Kilskeery until after the Feis in August last. Miss
Amy Barry, to whose work I referred in my last article,
came to Kilskeery three weeks before the Feis, and took
up the work when Miss Adrien left. Besides these the
committee sent Mr. Peter Kavanagh, a horticultural in-
structor, in April, and he only left in October. T
have also referred to his work in my last article. The
special deep trenching of the four school gardens was
done under Mr. Kavanagh’s supervision by voluntary
labour, the labour mostly of young men connected with
the Gaelic League in the parish. The instructor gave
instruction in horticulture to the boys of the different
Gaelic League classes in the parish; and to the children
of the four National Schools, close to which the gardens
ars situate. Mr. Kavanagh gave practical demonstra-
tions in manuring (by the way, the manure for all the
gardens was given free by parishioners), seed sowing,
and weeding on one, and sometimes on two, days a
week to each school from April on until October. ~ As
these school gardens were a mnew institution the work
was very heavy for the first year; early next spring
operations will begin again. Mr. Kavanagh also gave
advice to anyone who wanted it in the parish, and to
a considerable extent his services were availed of;
several people, as a result of this branch of activity,
made new gardens, or improved gardens already
existing.

The grown girls, and the girls going to school, are
learning to cook the vegetables as well as other things;
domestic economy is taught in nearly all the schools of
the parish, and Father Maguire hopes that shortly it

~will be taught in all without exception. There are

eleven schools in the parish, and Father Maguire is
manager of seven, for Kilskeery is in the North, where
non-Catholics are numerous. Domestic Economy is a
term that may cover a multitude of things. In Kil-
skeery, home sewing, including plain dressmaking,
repairs and alterations, laundry work, cookery and
general housewifery are taught by Miss Barry. The
gixls’ evening schools, at which these useful things are
taught, are carried on from April to September.  The
session for such classes consists of 70 lessons, three in
the week. Tn Trillick school there was a roll of about
32 and an average of 20, and Knocknagor had a roll of
45 and an average of 30 during the whole session. The
grown girls’ evening classes were examined by the
Nat_lonal Board Inspector in Reading, Writing, Arith-
metic, Irish and Domestic Science, and they made quite
a brilliant examination. Over forty turned up in
Knocknagor and about 25 in Trillick school, and they
all passed in all subjects, including Irish. From
February to April Miss Adrien taught two classes for
grown girls in Trillick, one from 10 to 12, and another
in the afternoon. Miss Barry is teaching now in most
of the National Schools, so she is kept busy, for she has
to teach in several places every day. There are Satur-
day classes in the winter season in Knocknagor from 12
to 2, and also in another centre for girls. Between the
Masses on Sunday—say, from 9.30 to 11.30, Irish
hymns and Irish prayers are taught in Kilskeery school.

Mr. Waldron has been in the parish since September,
1906, and as Father Maguire only took charge in July
of last year he did not waste much time until he secured
the services of an efficient native-speaking teacher.
Irish is taught in the day schools as an extra subject
outside the working hours.  Miss Annie Hession came
to the parish early in August last. She is a certificated
teacher from the Partry school. Mr. W. T. Grainger
first learned Irish in Dromore in 1904 ; his progress was
so rapid that in 1906 he obtained the Board’s certifi-
cale for the teaching of Irish as an extra subject, and
the bi-lingual programme. He also won a scholarship
at the Cloghaneely Training College during the session,
1906, at the close of which he obtained a first-class cer-
tificate. ~ He commenced teaching Irish in Kilskeery
parish in October of last year. Two of the N.T.’s in
the parish, Mr. John McBride, of Kilskeery, and Mr.
John Colten, of Knocknagor, have also qualified as Irish
teachers—both of them started Irish in Dromore.

In the evening schools Reading, Writing and Arith-
metic, a period of Irish and English history and the
Trish language are the subjects; in the evening classes
for grown girls, to which I have already referred,
Domestic Science takes the place of history.

Father Maguire speaks highly of the teachers and
their spirit and of the Irish spirit now prevailing in the
parish. Whether he had better materials to work with
in Kilskeery than are to be found in the average rural
English-speaking parish, north and south, I cannot say.
Nearly all now, old and young, have Irish salutations on
tkeir lips. There are upwards of nine hundred persons
actually learning Irish in the various classes, whilst
many not yet of school-going age and many of the older
people are to some extent learning it in their own homes
from some member or members of their families. It is
the Irish that keeps the steam up; even the pots and
pans of the cookery classes would grow cold and
neglected, in Father Maguire’s judgment, only for the
Trish. ‘Tt is the magnet; it is the soul of the whole
scheme,’’ said Father Maguire, ‘“and I won’t be satis-
fied until Kilskeery is a native-speaking parish.”’

In a few weeks the people raised over £70 to start the
Gaelic League operations, and, though almost within a
year since Father Maguire’s advent in the parish there
was a great Feis, the parish sent £8 to the Language
Fund. Whatever others may say against the National

Board, Father Maguire has no reason to complain of

it. Kilskeery got £98 b5s. from it by way of fees
for its five evening classes in which Irish is taught; it
also got £9 in book prizes, £2 each for four schools and

_£1 for the fifth, and they are expecting full fees-also

for the girls’ evening classes at which Irish was taught
also. Altogether the evening classes. are estimated to

are two hurling teams and one football team in the

over the borders of the parish—in Tummery, Dromore
—a string band is kept in being. : e

My readers, I hope, have now a fairly vivid idea of
the work of Irishisation in Kilskeery. About a year
ago it was an English-speaking parish, though whispers
of the doings of ‘‘ Irish Dromore ’’ may have reached
it. What is being done in Kilskeery might be at-
tempted in every English-speaking parish in Ireland.
However, I don’t wish to hold up my Kilskeery friend
as a headline to every P.P. in Ireland. It would not
be reasonable. Father Maguire is a man possessed with
a strong, vital Irish-Treland idea; he is an exceptional
man, as men of strong convictions allied with active
habits are. He has found out the secret forces of the
Trish language revival, and he has harnessed them to his
industrial and home brightening schemes; many men,
P.P.’s and laymen, quite as earnest as Father Maguire
for industry and home brightening, have struggled
without much effect, not seeing that Irish is the essen-
tial inspiration and spring of normal activity in Ireland ;
indeed, I believe there are sincere men who still look
upon Irish as a thing that it would be well to wipe off
the slate. ~ Father Maguire has insight into Ireland,
and others are not necessarily to be blamed if they lack
similar insight. It is for Irish-Ireland rather to en-
courage and help such men as Father Maguire and his
workers in Kilskeery than to concentrate an attack on
others who unhappily have not his brains and his fer-
vour. The Irish-Ireland movement is now a vigorous
organism, and it is for all of us to help its growth where
it is growing freely and not make distracting demon-
strations over incidental impediments to its growth in
stray places. When Kilskeery is quite an Irish native-
speakn}g parish, when it has a beautiful new church,
a spacious parish hall, a prosperous and independent
population, many who are doubters to-day may make
a visit of inquiry to it to:morrow. I have done with
Kilskeery for the present, and T cease writing of it with
regret. Now that the eyes of Trish-Treland have been
turned to it. there is an added responsibility on the
parish, and it has got a high reputation fo live up to.
Kilskeery is a headline to a hundred other parishes in
Ireland. When a long Anglicised, go-with-the-stream
parish says—‘ But what can we do?’’ the obvious
answer is—Haven't you heard what Kilskeery in the
county of Tyrone is doing—go and do likewise, or try

HOW THE MONEY GOES IN RATHMINES.

£9,4.59 13s. 0d. is a large sum! It was'spent by the
TRathmines Urban District Council in ‘“ Parliamentary
: and .Law Costs ” during 1905-6, in a vain effort to pass
2 Bill which ultimately was thrown out by a majority
of 210. For this huge sum of £9,459 13s. 0d., not one
particle of benefit came to the ratepayers whose money
was spent. For them, the money was utterly lost.

The items which make up the £9,459 13s. 0d. are
only now being audited. Previous to the regular audit
by the Local Government Board Auditor, the rate-
payers have a legal right to inspect the books, vouchers,
etc., which show how the money has been spent—their
money—the ratepayers. It is not much of a privilege,
but it is something. Article 27 of the *“ Order Book ’’
1s quite clear as to the right to inspect the accounts. It
Tuns as follows :—‘ The Secretary or Clerk shall, seven
clear days before the day for auditing the accounts of
the Council, deposit the abstract, half-yearly or yearly
statements, books, vouchers, and other documents re-
lating to the accounts, for inspection in the offices of
the Council, and shall permit the same, after they shall
have been so deposited, and before the day appointed
for the audit, to be inspected, and if it be desired,
-copied, by all persons interested during office hours
when the Council shall not be setting.”’

Nothing could be plainer. The writer of this article
and another ratepayer duly attended. We were
anxious to see how the £9,459 13s. 0d.—ratepayers’
money—had been spent on ‘ Law Costs.”” TUnder the
‘heading, ‘‘ Witnesses’ Expenses,” we found a great
many names of witnesses, and after each name a sum
of money. In some cases there was nothing more—
-absolutely nothing. In other cases the two or three
-smaller amounts which made up the total amount were
given, but without explanation. There was no state-
ment—absolutely none—as to whether the amounts
were for travelling charges, or for hotel expenses, or
as fees. Above all, there were no vouchers, absolutely
none. As this was directly contrary to the provisions
of Article 27, we thought it necessary to ask, formally,
for more information, and in reply were told, in writ-
ing, by the Clerk that ‘‘the said vouchers’’ had not
‘been in his custody ‘‘ since they were submitted to the
Taxing Master of the House of Commons.”’

So much for Article 27, and the right of ratepayers
‘to know how their money has been spent!

Even the particulars—without details and without
vouchers—are interesting. We give some:—

WEXFORD AGAIN LEADS.

GREAT TEMPERANCE CRUSADE.

HE Diocese of Ferns has befittingly commemorated
the Golden Jubilee of the foundation of the
* Ferns Temperance movement, established by Bishop

Furlong, by a great Temperance crusade that has just

been brought to a successful conclusion. In the Pas-

toral letter of the present Bishop of Ferns a suggestion

= was thrown out to the effect that this year—the Golden

Jubilee year of Dr. Furlong’s Temperance Organisation

- —-should not be let pass by without some appropriate

celebration. The Priests’ Anti-Treating and Temper-

ance League Committee set to work and arranged the
details of the October crusade.

Objects of Crusade.—(a) To commemorate the Golden
Jubilee of the foundation of the Ferns Temperance move-
ment and to honour the memory of the founder, Dr.
Furlong; (b) To re-invigorate the Anti-Treating move-
ment throughout the diocese; (¢) To promote total ab-
stinence amongst all boys and girls under twenty-one,
=8 and encourage it amongst all others; (4) To combat

i and, if possible, entirely suppress drinking at threshings

and haymaking; (¢) To inculcate a stricter observance
of Dr. Furlong’s salutary law, now fifty years in force,
against the purchase and sale of intoxicants on Sundays
and holydays; (f) and, finally, to deal with serious

g drinking abuses of whatever kind found to prevail in

the locality.

Detailed Arrangements.—As not only unity of pur-
pose, but also uniformity of plan were very necessary
for the success of the work, the Committee thought it
advisable to enter into particulars as to ways and means,
and drew up a programme of work which was closely
followed. In addition to the Fathers of the House of
Missions, Enniscorthy, twelve of the most distinguished
members of the Redemptorist Order and two Jesuit
Fathers were engaged, and during -the month - now

* ended they bini et bini have passed from parish to
- parish instructing, preaching, hearing confessions and
- administering pledges to thousands of Wexford Catho-
lics. Special instructions were given two mornings in
each church to the juveniles to whom the Total Abstin-
ence Pledge was given with all possible solemnity, and
thousands .of whom may be now seen wearing the little
" a'uminium badge. The Anti-Treating Pledge has been
taken so extensively that the supply of badges has run
- out.
 Drink at Threshings.—Although not equally bad in
~ all districts, the custom of supplying large quantities of
- intoxicating liquor at threshings was generally recog-
nised as one of the worst drinking customs in the
- diocese.  The quantity of drink consumed had been
steadily increasing year by year. One priest, after con-
sultation with the farmers, calculated that about £250
j spent on drink at these farming operations in his

parish last year. Taking that as an average parish we
find that a sum equal to £10,000 is the annual expen-
diture in the Diocese of Ferns on drink under this head-
ing. So firm was the hold that drink on these occasions
had taken that it could not be successfully combatted,
otherwise than by forming a strong combination against
it. That, thanks to the Temperance crusade, has been
done by establishing in every parish a

LEAGUE OF HEADS OF FAMILIES.

This being a new Temperance League we give its aims *
and objects as we find them in a leaflet issued by the
Rev. John Rossiter, M.S.S., on bhehalf of the Tem-
perance Committee. :

“ Inasmuch as the custom of distributing intoxicating
liquor at certain farming operations, particularly
threshing and hayrick-making, has now become a very
serious abuse, is leading to much unnecessary and exces-
sive drinking, is a great snare for our young boys, who
in such temptation often break the Confirmation Pledge
and begin to drink, and is consequently a grave cause
of demoralisation to families:—

‘“We, heads of houses, both farmers and farm
labourers, deem it a conscientious duty to unmite in
God’s name for the suppression of this abuse.”’

Farmers’ Promise.—Therefore, we, the undersigned
farmers, acting purely from a sense of duty, make this
deliberate promise, and pledge our word of honour for
its faithful fulfilment: —

1st. That we will not in future give intoxicating
liquor to anyone on the occasion of threshing and hay-
rick-making, neither to those we employ, nor to neigh-
bours who come to assist us.

2nd. That neither we ourselves nor members of our
families nor our servants will assist at threshings or
hayrick-making where intoxicating drink is supplied.

Labourers’ Promise.—We, the undersigned labouring
men—ourselves heads of families—heartily welcome this
combined movement to remove from our midst so great
a source of temptation, danger and sin.

We promise to give it our full support, as being well
caleulated to promote a higher standard of sobriety,
efficiency and respectability amongst our own body ;
and as being a most necessary safeguard for the young
boys of our families for whose welfare we are responsible:
to God. :

““’Tis God and Country or Porter.”’—In these words
a good old Wexford farmer summed up the duty of
Wexfordmen. Thanks to the good work of the Mis-
sionary Fathers and the religious and patriotic feelings
of Wexfordmen, the combination against this drinking
abuse has succeeded beyond all expectations. Hundreds
of heads of families in every parish have signed these
two pledges, and are acting up to their promise. Some
weak-kneed farmers, afraid they could not get their
work done, produced the ‘‘quarter cask,’” with the
result that, in at least two or three cases, the men re-
fused to work and marched off with their pikes upon:
their shoulders. During the crusade the Rosary was.
recited in Irish in many parishes.

WHAT MIGHT BE_DONE TO REGENERATE
ADARE.

C‘ PEAKING at the meeting in connection with the
. ) delivery of the auditor’s address at University
College a few years ago, Captain Shawe-Taylor used’
the following words:—‘‘ If T were not a Galway man I
should wish to be a Limerick man.”” This statement
was pure plimar but was made—so the speaker said’
—on account of all that Limerick men, notably Lord
Dunraven, had done to end the land war. I am sure
that were it not for the tender susceptibilities of the
Galwegians the gallant captain would have gone further,
and said that he wished he had been born in Adare so
as to qualify for the honour of being a neighbour of:
Lord Dunraven, -

Adare has, certainly, loomed large in contemporary
pelitics.  Most people seem to think that the name
belongs to a happy and thriving town—a sort of ‘ Sweet
'Al_lbul‘n *’ in this age of ‘‘ Ireland’s griefs.”” Perhaps
1t is as flourishing as most Irish towns similarly situated,
but that does not mean much. The town is pretty
enough, and the surroundings beautiful, but the rank
green grass—that unmistakable sign of decay, as
General Butler recently pointed out—is the outstanding
feature in the country, no matter from what direction
the town is approached. Adare, like Kilkenny, has a
famous history. I am ashamed to confess that few of
its present tranquil inhabitants know little of this his-
“tory—who occupied its noble monastic institutions, or
how they and the great stronghold of the Desmonds in
‘the place rose and fell.

Judged by the standard of population, Adare is
ﬁecadent-, indeed. It numbered over a thousand souls
in the forties of the last century, now only about four
hundred and fifty. At the former time the town had a
national reputation as a centre of the cider-making in-
-dustry, mow it neither makes nor consumes that
beverage. The puzzle is, why some profitable industry,
which should give large employment, is not started in
the place, more especially as such an able, wealthy and
sympathetic a man as Lord Dunraven lives there. One
would imagine that, in this time of language and ir.dus-
trial revival, the Adare folk, with Lord Dunraven at
their head, would re-establish cider manufacture on a
large scale. It is eminently feasible. There are still
:scme splendid orchards in the neighbourhood, although,
unfortunately, some of the finest have disappeared. To
make good the loss the Department of Agriculture
might be asked to intervene, and supply the trees for
planting, as it has done in other parts of the country.
‘Then why not grow gooseberries, currants, plums, etc.,
and vegetables in addition to apples? The soil and
«climate of the locality are most suitable for the pur-
pose, and a supply of cheap labour is to be had. The
tenant farmers of yesterday are the peasant proprietors
-of to-day, and, consequently, can bestir themselves and
-direct their energies to anything profitable in the way
-of horticulture without fearing that ‘‘ the rent will be
raised.”’

If cider-making is started on a commercial scale, it
might be argued that the men would be engaged at it
for only seven months, and be idle for the remainder
of the year. This will not hold good, as the roads in
'Co. Limerick are worked by direct labour at the very
period, say, from November to May, when the cider
presses are not being used. e

Next, it should be quite easy to establish a splendid
furniture factory in Adare. It is surrounded on all
sides by fine woods of ash, elm, beech and oak, as one
could easily infer from a glance at the Irish form of
-the name of the town— &¢-Oaqa.  There are several
-skilled workmen in the village or its immediate neigh-
‘bourhood—in fact, the people there have undoubted
talent in the field of mechanical art, so that 1t should
take only a very brief time to train a band of local
.cabinet-makers. Lord Dunraven has a saw-mil] work-
:ing on his estate, and, moreover, is credited with being
-quite willing to expend money on any project which
will materially benefit his people.  Recently he has
-experimented in  tobacco-growing with, I have been
teld, highly satisfactory results. I am sure that his
‘many. duties have hitherto -prevented his seeing how
easily he could establish a great furniture factory in
the town. Captain Otway Cuffe has done so in Kil-
‘kenny under greater difficulties. It should be a com-
paratively simple matter to- find a market for the
“finished goods, inasmuch as Adare is in direct rail com-
munication with Limerick, Ennis, Galway, Tralee,

“Clonmel, Waterford, and, practically, with Mallow and

Now we turn to the fields of employment for girls.
I¢ housekeeping is excepted there are none such to be
found in Adare. Under the present circumstances all
the girls cannot be mistresses of establishments of their
own. More’s the pity, for the homg is, pre-eminently,
woman’s sphere. ~ But while our cider-making, fruit
culture and furniture manufacture are progressing—as
they must—cannot we find somqthing to do for our”
grown girls? Father Matt Maguire has done wonders
up in Dromore, and Father O’Hara in Kiltimagh.
Surely we laymen will not be behind.

Limerick lace and crochet are world-famed. Neither
the one nor the other is made in Adare. But I have
seen some fancy needlework done by girls at the Con-
vent School under the supervision of the good nuns
which was really charming. Those who can do embroi-
dery work well can assuredly learn the art of lace and
crochet making. Father Maguire and Father O’Hara
experimented successfully in establishing lace schools,
ete., in ¢ Irish Dromore’’ and Kiltimagh, and shall
iv be said that we keen, intelligent Southerners cannot
emulate them or beat them?

Having started our girls at lace and crochet work,
we may have some less deft-fingered sisters to provide
for. Well, these can be set to hosiery and shirt-making.
Although we humble folk cannot afford to buy lace
very extensively, we certainly must purchase stockings
and such like. But we could rely on Lord Dunraven’s
many wealthy visitors every year as regular purchasers
of the more delicate handiwork of our women-workers.

We revivalists must look after the mental as well as
the material well-being of our people. Consequently,
one of the first things we must do is to establish a
branch of the Gaelic League in ‘‘ Sweet Adare,”” for
there is no such thing there at present. Seagdn Ua
Tuama and his boon companions—the Maigue Poets of
whom An o-ozan Paopaig Ua Oumnnin has lately
teld us so much—should be amazed if to-day they could
stroll along the banks of the Maigue from Croom to
Adare, for they would not hear a word of their well-
loved Irish spoken in either place. Is not this our dis-
grace?

Heaven knows, Adare is dull by day and much more
so by night. Apart from reading, little that is con-
genial or pleasant is to be done there once the sun sets.
And few of us read or utilise the good library at the
Christian Brothers’ Schools. Knowing this, who cun
wender if the young men—and old—make their way to
one or other of the six public-houses in the place?
There is far too much drinking, but we shall ten, if
not kill, the drink evil by the establishment of a village
hall where the Irish language, dancing and music could
be taught. We, Adare men, are patriotic in our sub-
conscious way. We can boast—and do boast—that
there are not more than two or three men from our
town in the British army. Hence we should gladly
avail ourselves of the opportunity of acquiring our lan-
guage—our great national characteristic.

We had a good brass band a few years ago. The in-
struments still remain! The revival of the band should
not be impossible.

As to field sports, Adare has nome. Yet she pro-
duced a champion of the world at long distance running,
and had a-famous hurling team at one time. The
club that conquered the ‘“ Adare Desmonds’’ in the
eighties justly regarded itself as having done ““a big
thing.”” But the music of the crashing hurlies is heard

‘But so much for the school and its prospectus. I
must not forget to say a word about the Gaelic League
outside the school. And here I don’t know that I can
be so complimentary or so flattering. T wish the Gaelic
League proper would get out a prospectus, too. It is
not too easy to get to know anything about it. I was
present at the annual meeting some weeks back. There
was a very excellent and convincing lecture delivered
as to the wisdom, etc., of learning Irish, but somehow
nobody seemed to button-hole you and ask you to join
a class. There were no leaflets distributed.  There
was no mention of any particular class commencing
work on any particular day or week. You felt that
you were supposed to know all about these things or
else that you were expected to make your way up to
some of the few people on the platform and get infor-
mation, if they had it to give, from them. You could,
of course, if a stranger, become suddenly interested in
the Gaelic League to such an extent that you would go
to the trouble of ferretting out the address of its head-
quarters and call round there that night late, or next
day, for particulars. But ordinary people don’t do
that sort of thing. They expect to be saved such
trouble. They expect to be, at least, asked to join. I
believe I understood that the city branches were not
just yet ready to open business for the winter. If I
understood rightly, T wonder what the meeting was
supposed to be the opening meeting of ?

However, all that is perhaps a small matter ; perhaps
the idea is that the branches will in due course blow
each its own horn and set about commandeering us as
members. I hope so. I should like to see them vieing
with each other as propagandists as well as in the
matter of learning Irish. If they did they would, for

• der the police capable of giving the largest possible re-

STATE OF AGRICULTURE.

A SUGGESTION.

2| HERE are two sections in the International Ex-
hibition which afford much food for thought re-
garding the possibilities ahead of us, along the two
main lines of economic progress—the Industrial and
Agricultural.

Passing through the section devoted to Irish indus-
tries (having noted the excellent native products shown
in other sections) one is forced to ponder thus:—When
Ireland can produce all these things, with so much suc-
cess, and in face of keen competition with her foreign
vivals, how is it she cannot do-so a thousand fold?
Why does she import millions’ worth of similar goods
anuually, while her sons and daughters emigrate by the
hundred thousand?  Where are her captains of in-
dustry? Why do they not come forth to rouse up her
white-livered capitalists, not to a sense of their duty, but
to the value of their opportunities ?

A similar trend of thought is suggested by a visit to
the Agricultural section, or, rather, the example plots
which are run by certain chemical manure manufac-
turers to show the value of their products. One por-
tion of these plots illustrates the merits of sulphate of
ammonia. The courteous expert in charge (who is a
practical farmer from Co. Antrim) gives a most interest-
ing and instructive history of his plots ; pointing out the
abundance of the crops on those which had had a dress-
ing of ammonia sulphate’ compared with the meagre
yield on those alongside which had been treated
similarly in every other respect. The gist of his in-
formation went to show that, by a trifling expenditure
on artificial manures (carefully selected and properly
applied), our farmers might increase the produce of
their farms from thirty to fifty per cent. And, be it
noted, this increase comes under only one head of im-
proved agriculture—manuring.

Now, let us view it under another important head,
that is, the quality of seeds. On this point I gleaned
some astonishing facts quite recently from a most re-
liable source. I came across a young Ulsterman who
had come up to Dublin to compete in an exam. for the
Department’s Agricultural Scholarships at the Royal
College of Science—a splendid type of the youthful
Northern student-farmer, hardy, intelligent, self-con-
fident, and yet modest. Being brought up on a small
farm, in humble circumstances, he could speak from
actual personal knowledge. Naturally the conversation
turned on agriculture, the Department in its relation
thereto, and, in short, the general industrial prospect.
““ Look here,”” T said, ‘“ some of our leading economists
have stated, over and over again, that by the adoption
of modern methods our farmers could at least double
the present produce of their farms. Now tell me, can-
didly, what is your view of this theory?’’ ‘ That it is
thoroughly sound,” he replied. “‘Let me give you a
few facts bearing on this point,”” he continued. ‘ Last
spring T procured grass seeds in time fo send a sample
to the Department to have its germinating power tested,
and found that over fifty per cent. of it was utterly
bhad! Later I had mangold seed tested, and learned
that little over a third of it was capable of growing!
Now, in the ordinary course of things, our farmers sow
such seeds without question, and when the result is a
poor, miserable crop they blame the weather, the flies,
the grubs—anything rather than the proper cause. Had
I sown the grass seeds referred to it would have entailed
not only the loss of half a crop of hay, but half the
amount of grazing during the period until the ground
is broken again for tillage.”

Discussing his experience of itinerant instructors, he:
expressed the view that their work is not having the:
results that were hoped for; their lectures, though ex-
cellent, have not the driving force necessary to overcome:
the inertia of the average farmer; and, if substantial
progress is to be made, some additional means must be-
employed. 3 R

Considering the vital importance of apply_mg improved
methods to agriculture, would it not be well if some of our*
county committees drew up a scheme for instituting
premium farms—in other words, scholarships in modern
farming? They might be assigned to wxde_ly separated
districts, and tenable, on approved conditions, for a
period of four years, in order to bring th}a student-
farmer through the full course gf crop rotation.  The
trifling sum of £480 would provide four scholarships of
£30 each for a period of four years. 3

The accepted candidate should agree to place himself
ontirvely in the hands of the County Inst}'uctor; and
everything done on the farm should be publl§hed broad-
cast. e should he an average member of his class, but
intelligent, industrious, and progressive ; a'm_d his reten-
tion of the premium might be made conditional on the
amount of actual results he would be able to show. In
addition to the main subject, marks might be given for
general neatness of homestead and farm, _culbivatiou of
fruit and vegetables, absence of weeds, trim hedgerows,
good gates and passes, etc. ¥

Some such scheme as this would have many points to
commend it.

In, the first place it would bring scientific
agriculture home to the farmers of a whole dis-
trict, in its most convincing form, through the medium
of one of their own class, and on a farm similar to their
own; and, unlike the case of mere example plots, the
results would be sufficiently tangible to fasten on the
popular mind and rouse a spirit of emulation, even
from motives of self-interest ; secondly, the money grant
would enable the student-farmer to bear the initial ex-
pense of taking up improved methods—say, for addi-
tional labour, implements, manures, seeds, etc.; and,
lastly, when the premium should pass to another dis-
trict, this model farm might be expceted to maintain its
Ligh standard, and thus serve as an example to the
whole country around it.

These few rambling thoughts and suggestions spring
from a sincere desire to see an advance in our produc-
tive methods, and are put forward with a view to draw
public attention to a most important subject ; and that
it is one which still requires much consideration is
evidenced by the fact that,” though the Department and
county committees are at work over half-a-dozen years,
Ireland’s tillage has fallen off by 40,698 acres during
the past twelve months.
